{{Short description="Massive scalar boson responsible for the mass of elementary particles}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Higgs boson
| image = Candidate Higgs Events in ATLAS and CMS.png
| caption = Candidate Higgs boson events from [[ATLAS experiment|ATLAS]] (left) and [[CMS experiment|CMS]] (right) at the [[Large Hadron Collider]].
| topics = [[Standard Model]], [[Boson]], [[Elementary particle]], [[W and Z bosons]], [[Fundamental interaction]]
| composition = [[Elementary particle]]
| statistics = [[Boson]]
| mass = 125.11 ± 0.11 GeV/c²
| charge = 0
| spin = 0
| discovered = 2012, [[Large Hadron Collider]]
}}

The '''Higgs boson''' is an [[elementary particle]] in the [[Standard Model]] of [[particle physics]]. It is the quantum excitation of the [[Higgs field]] — a fundamental field that permeates all of [[spacetime]]. The Higgs boson has [[spin (physics)|spin]] 0 (a scalar boson), no [[electric charge]], and a [[mass]] of approximately 125 GeV/c².

The discovery of the Higgs boson at [[CERN]]'s [[Large Hadron Collider]] (LHC) on 4 July 2012 was a landmark event in physics, leading to the [[Nobel Prize in Physics]] in 2013 for [[François Englert]] and [[Peter Higgs]].

== The Higgs mechanism ==
{{Main|Higgs mechanism}}
In the Standard Model, the [[Higgs field]] spontaneously breaks the [[electroweak theory|electroweak symmetry]] via the [[Brout–Englert–Higgs mechanism]]:

* Before symmetry breaking: massless W and Z bosons, massless fermions
* After symmetry breaking: W⁺, W⁻, Z⁰ acquire mass (~80–91 GeV/c²); fermions acquire mass through [[Yukawa interaction|Yukawa couplings]] to the Higgs field

The photon remains massless because it couples only to the unbroken part of the symmetry.

== Analogy ==
A common analogy: the Higgs field is like a room full of people. A celebrity (a massive particle) entering the room attracts a cluster and moves slowly — acquiring "mass." A less-known person (a low-mass particle) moves through easily. The Higgs boson is like a rumour passing through the room — the clustering itself propagates.

== Properties ==
* Mass: 125.11 ± 0.11 GeV/c² (~133× proton mass)
* Mean lifetime: ~1.6×10⁻²² s
* Decay modes: <math>H \rightarrow b\bar{b}</math> (58%), <math>H \rightarrow WW^*</math> (21%), <math>H \rightarrow \gamma\gamma</math> (0.2%), etc.
* Self-coupling predicted but not yet directly measured

== Production at the LHC ==
Higgs bosons are produced primarily through:
* [[Gluon fusion]] (<math>gg \rightarrow H</math>) — dominant
* Vector boson fusion
* Associated production with W/Z bosons or top quarks

== Significance ==
* Completes the Standard Model's particle content
* Explains why some particles have mass while others don't
* The [[hierarchy problem]] — why the Higgs mass is so light (125 GeV) rather than at the Planck scale — motivates theories beyond the Standard Model ([[supersymmetry]], [[compositeness]])

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Boson]]
* [[Electroweak theory]]
* [[Higgs mechanism]]
* [[Large Hadron Collider]]
* [[Standard Model]]
* [[W and Z bosons]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Higgs boson| ]]
[[Category:Bosons]]
[[Category:Elementary particles]]
[[Category:Standard Model]]
